one_line_takeaway: By tuning LIF spiking-neuron hyperparameters (V_thr, tau) instead of poisoning input data, BadSNN embeds backdoors in SNNs that survive fine-tuning, TSBD, and ANP-style pruning defenses far better than BadNet/Blend/WaNet baselines, at the cost of being defeatable by NAD on some (but not all) datasets.
key_finding: BadSNN achieves a favorable CA/ASR trade-off at moderate attack hyperparameters (V_thr^a approx 1.10-1.15): e.g. for CIFAR-10 at V_thr^a=1.10, tau^a=0.5 the model reaches CA=87.22% (vs. Clean CA=91.34%, Base CA=87.68%) with ASR_p=77.79% and ASR_o=82.65%; GTSRB reaches CA=92.57% with ASR_o=75.59%; CIFAR-100 reaches CA=60.91% with ASR_p=73.88%/ASR_o=57.20%; N-MNIST reaches CA=94.06% with ASR_p=100%. Crucially, BadSNN is markedly more robust to defenses than the three conventional trigger-poisoning baselines: after Fine-Tuning, BadSNN retains ASR_o=84.55% on CIFAR-10 (vs. baseline ASRs dropping to 16.85-34.0%); against TSBD it retains ASR_o=82.61% (CIFAR-10) and 84.99% (GTSRB), while TSBD drops all three baselines below ~20% ASR; against ANP it retains ASR near or above baseline levels but at heavy CA cost (e.g. CA collapses to 28.93% on CIFAR-10, 11.05% on GTSRB, 1.07% on CIFAR-100 while ASR stays 98-100%), which the paper attributes to entanglement of backdoor and clean neurons from dual-spike learning rather than a discrete trigger-associated neuron subset. NAD is the one defense that substantially reduces BadSNN's ASR on GTSRB (9.86%) and CIFAR-100 (7.4%), but fails on CIFAR-10 (ASR_o=81.89% after NAD). Ablations show: (1) malicious training hyperparameters need V_thr^t well above nominal V_thr^n=1.0 (V_thr^t=1.5 gives the most flexible CA/ASR landscape; V_thr^t=0.8 makes finding a good attack-time operating point very hard, Fig. 3); (2) poisoning-ratio effect is non-monotonic -- 1%, 3%, 5% poison ratios give strong performance while 2% and 4% show large ASR drops despite preserved CA (Fig. 4a); (3) ASR_o saturates near 99% at a perturbation magnitude of only ~2.0 for the optimized trigger, beyond which L2 distortion grows roughly linearly for little extra ASR gain (Fig. 4b); (4) the attack transfers from LIF to learnable Parametric LIF (PLIF) neurons, with PLIF retaining higher CA under attack on CIFAR-10 but comparable CA/ASR on CIFAR-100 and GTSRB (Fig. 5).
core_contribution: BadSNN, a backdoor attack on spiking neural networks (SNNs) that embeds the backdoor entirely by manipulating the LIF (leaky integrate-and-fire) spiking neuron hyperparameters -- the membrane potential threshold V_thr and membrane time constant tau -- during training, rather than by poisoning training data with an explicit trigger pattern or flipping labels. The authors call this 'malicious spike poisoning': during backdoor training a subset of target-label samples is trained under both nominal spiking-neuron settings (V_thr^n, tau^n) and malicious settings (V_thr^t, tau^t) via a 'dual spike learning' objective, teaching the model to associate elevated/out-of-distribution spike counts with the target label. A second contribution is a trigger optimization procedure: a U-Net-based surrogate trigger-generation model (T_o) is trained with a combined cosine-similarity, adversarial, and weighted-MSE loss to synthesize minimally perceptible image perturbations that push spike activity into the out-of-distribution range and activate the backdoor at inference, improving on an initial nonlinear power-transformation trigger (T_p, x^q) that is more perceptible/semantics-degrading. For neuromorphic (event-based, DVS) data a separate trigger T_s adds bounded uniform random perturbations (beta=0.03) directly to spike frames. The paper further shows the attack generalizes from fixed LIF to learnable Parametric LIF (PLIF) neurons.
threat_model: Adopted from prior backdoor-attack literature (BadNets, LOTUS, WaNet threat models): white-box adversary with full control over the training process (a malicious trainer or model/dataset provider), who wants to maximize attack success rate while preserving clean-task utility. The key departure from the conventional setting is that the attacker does not need to poison the training data with mislabeled/triggered samples -- instead they manipulate the spiking neuron's hyperparameters (V_thr, tau) during training. The trigger function itself is constructed and optimized after backdoor training completes (not predefined beforehand), and at inference the attacker must additionally choose intermediate 'attack' hyperparameters (V_thr^a, tau^a) that lie between the nominal and malicious training values to jointly satisfy correct clean-label behavior and target-label behavior under the trigger (Eq. 8).
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged/observable from the text: NAD (attention-distillation fine-tuning) does meaningfully suppress BadSNN on GTSRB and CIFAR-100, so the attack's defense-robustness is dataset-dependent, not universal. ANP defeats BadSNN's ASR only at the cost of destroying clean accuracy (e.g. CA=28.93% on CIFAR-10, 1.07% on CIFAR-100), which the paper frames as a defense failure but which a defender might also treat as an effective (if blunt) mitigation. The relationship between poisoning ratio and attack success is reported as non-linear/non-monotonic with no explanatory mechanism offered beyond 'empirical calibration.' The trigger-optimization pipeline (T_o) and the DeepFool-based target-perturbation search are demonstrated only for static-image datasets; for neuromorphic data the much simpler random-perturbation trigger T_s is used instead because the optimization approach 'cannot be directly applied' to event data, so the two data modalities are not evaluated under a matched attack pipeline. All experiments use one fixed timestep count (T=4) and the DSPIKE surrogate gradient; robustness to STRIP-style poisoned-sample detection or Neural-Cleanse-style trigger-inversion defenses (both mentioned in Related Work) is not evaluated, only pruning- and fine-tuning-based defenses are tested. Attacker requires white-box, full-training-process control, a strong assumption consistent with prior backdoor literature but that narrows real-world applicability to supply-chain/malicious-trainer scenarios.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 (Spiking ResNet-19, V_thr^a=1.10/tau^a=0.5): Clean CA=91.34%, Base CA=87.68%, CA=87.22%, ASR_p=77.79%, ASR_o=82.65%
GTSRB (Spiking VGG-16, V_thr^a=1.10/tau^a=0.5): Clean CA=96.05%, Base CA=93.02%, CA=92.57%, ASR_p=39.81%, ASR_o=75.59%
CIFAR-100 (Spiking VGG-16, V_thr^a=1.10/tau^a=0.5): Clean CA=71.85%, Base CA=65.19%, CA=60.91%, ASR_p=73.88%, ASR_o=57.20%
N-MNIST (N-MNIST Net, V_thr^a=1.10/tau^a=0.5): Clean CA=96.06%, Base CA=95.10%, CA=94.06%, ASR_p=100%

CIFAR-10 robustness (best result across defenses, Table II): BadSNN before-defense CA/ASR=87.22%/82.66%; after Fine-Tuning 82.25%/84.55%; after CLP 83.83%/72.59%; after ANP 28.93%/98.43%; after TSBD 71.27%/82.61%; after NAD 87.02%/81.89%
GTSRB robustness: BadSNN before-defense 87.43%/84.99%; after Fine-Tuning 38.70%/1.69%; after CLP 3.33%/100.0%; after ANP 11.05%/99.80%; after TSBD 87.65%/84.99%; after NAD 70.10%/9.86%
CIFAR-100 robustness: BadSNN before-defense 61.18%/74.23%; after Fine-Tuning 6.27%/16.49%; after CLP 66.73%/6.66%; after ANP 1.07%/100.0%; after TSBD 2.45%/53.09%; after NAD 5.9%/7.4%
Baseline BadNet on CIFAR-10 before defense: 91.05%/100% ASR, reduced to 34.94% ASR by CLP and 9.4% ASR by TSBD
Perturbation magnitude ablation (CIFAR-10, Spiking ResNet-19): ASR_o rises to 99% at perturbation magnitude ~2.0, with average L2 norm growing roughly linearly thereafter
Poisoning ratio ablation (CIFAR-10): ratios of 1%, 3%, 5% give strong CA/ASR; 2% and 4% show significant ASR drops despite preserved CA
LIF vs PLIF ablation at V_thr^a=1.15: LIF shows lower CIFAR-10 CA than PLIF under attack conditions, with comparable CA/ASR on CIFAR-100 and GTSRB
